themes.label: COMPOSITION
themes.tagline: Paper 1 Writing — 50 marks. Situational (14) + Continuous (36). Story Mountain, F.A.S.T., R.E.F., Weather Contrast, Flashback.

themes.label: GRAMMAR
themes.tagline: Paper 2 Booklet A + half of Editing. ~25 marks ride on tense, SVA, articles, prepositions, pronouns, conjunctions.

themes.label: VOCABULARY
themes.tagline: Paper 2 Booklet A (Vocab MCQ + Cloze). 170+ collocations, 80+ idioms, register matrix, 60+ connectors.

themes.label: COMPREHENSION
themes.tagline: Paper 2 Booklet A/B — 65 marks combined. Visual Text MCQ, Comprehension Cloze, Editing, S&T, Comprehension OE. RACE is the spine.

themes.label: ORAL & LISTENING
themes.tagline: Papers 3 & 4 — 60 marks. Listening 20 (read twice), Reading Aloud 15 (PACT preamble), SBC 25 (real photo, no longer linked to RA).

phenomena.label: F.A.S.T. lens
phenomena.blurb: Show emotion through Face, Actions, Speech, Thoughts — never name it flatly. The single most-used AL1 craft technique.

phenomena.label: R.E.F. ending
phenomena.blurb: Reflection + Emotions + Future-action. Avoids 'I learnt a valuable lesson'. Anchored in a specific image.

phenomena.label: Story Mountain (5-stage)
phenomena.blurb: Intro → Rising Action → Climax → Falling Action → Conclusion. Pacing matters — slow the climax.

phenomena.label: Weather Contrast technique
phenomena.blurb: Open with weather; mirror or ironically contrast inner state; close with the sky transformed. Used in 2 of 3 mentor essays.

phenomena.label: Flashback technique
phenomena.blurb: Open in the present (hospital, exam result), flash back to the incident, return to present. Use 'Let me take you back to…'.

phenomena.label: Mentor essays (3)
phenomena.blurb: Three Band-5 annotated essays in ModelEssays_Annotated_KB.md. Read each twice — once for story, once for craft.

phenomena.label: Banned phrases
phenomena.blurb: 'I learnt a valuable lesson', 'It was the best day of my life', 'Little did I know…'. These openers/closers signal AL2 immediately.

phenomena.label: Onomatopoeia (no quotes)
phenomena.blurb: Thwack! Piak! Whoosh! Sound words stand alone without quotation marks — they are not dialogue.

phenomena.label: Numbers under 10 spelled
phenomena.blurb: Write 'first', 'fifth', 'ten' — not '1st', '5th', '10'. Saves spelling-style penalties.

phenomena.label: P.A.C.W. (Sit Writing)
phenomena.blurb: Purpose · Audience · Context · Writer-persona. Lock all four in before drafting a single sentence.

phenomena.label: Critical-thinking point
phenomena.blurb: One of the 6 content points is underlined. Information is NOT in the stimulus — you must invent a realistic specific detail.

phenomena.label: 7 Situational genres
phenomena.blurb: Formal letter, informal email, report, speech, article (new 2025), review, notice. Each has its own salutation/sign-off.

phenomena.label: Salutation ↔ Sign-off pairs
phenomena.blurb: Dear Sir/Madam ↔ Yours faithfully. Dear Mr Tan ↔ Yours sincerely. Hi John ↔ Take care. Mismatches cost marks.

phenomena.label: Register (formal/informal)
phenomena.blurb: Genre + audience set the register. Get/ask/tell → obtain/enquire/inform when formal. Singlish leaks cost marks.

phenomena.label: Tense (past/present/perfect)
phenomena.blurb: Past simple, past continuous, past perfect, present perfect. Tense agreement across a passage is half the cloze battle.

phenomena.label: Subject-Verb Agreement
phenomena.blurb: Singular → singular; plural → plural. Neither/Either/Each + singular. One of + singular. Collective nouns vary.

phenomena.label: Articles a/an/the
phenomena.blurb: 'An' before vowel SOUNDS: an hour, a university. 'The' for specific; 'a/an' for non-specific.

phenomena.label: Verb-preposition pairs
phenomena.blurb: Depend ON, rely ON, succeed IN, agree WITH, apologise FOR. Fixed pairs — memorise them as units.

phenomena.label: Pronouns
phenomena.blurb: Subject (I, he, she, we), object (me, him, her, us), possessive (my, his, our). Avoid 'me and Jack played' → 'Jack and I played'.

phenomena.label: Conjunctions
phenomena.blurb: and, but, or, because, although, while. Don't double-up: 'Although it rained, but the match continued' is wrong.

phenomena.label: Make vs Do
phenomena.blurb: Make a decision, make progress, make an effort. Do homework, do research, do business. The classic confusion pair.

phenomena.label: Heavy / Strong / Light
phenomena.blurb: Heavy rain, heavy traffic. Strong coffee, strong wind. Light meal, light reading. Never 'strong rain'.

phenomena.label: Verb-preposition collocations
phenomena.blurb: Depend on, rely on, succeed in, consist of, suffer from. The Cloze tests 3-4 of these every paper.

phenomena.label: Idioms (80+, freq-tagged)
phenomena.blurb: On cloud nine, butterflies in stomach, face the music, at a crossroads. Use 1-2 per Continuous Writing piece — when they fit the moment.

phenomena.label: Connectors — Adding
phenomena.blurb: In addition, furthermore, moreover, also, besides. Vary; don't repeat the same connector twice in a paragraph.

phenomena.label: Connectors — Contrasting
phenomena.blurb: However, nevertheless, on the other hand, although, despite. Watch Despite + noun phrase; Although + clause.

phenomena.label: Connectors — Causal
phenomena.blurb: Because, since, due to, owing to, thanks to. 'Due to' takes a noun phrase; 'because' takes a clause.

phenomena.label: Connectors — Sequence
phenomena.blurb: First, next, then, meanwhile, finally, eventually, suddenly. Listening signal words too.

phenomena.label: Formal ↔ Informal swap pairs
phenomena.blurb: get→obtain, ask→enquire, tell→inform, start→commence, try→attempt, find out→discover.

phenomena.label: RACE framework
phenomena.blurb: Restate · Answer · Cite (exact quote in quotation marks) · Explain. Use on every 2+ mark OE question.

phenomena.label: Literal questions
phenomena.blurb: 'According to the text...'. Find the exact answer in the passage. Quote carefully — don't paraphrase.

phenomena.label: Inferential questions
phenomena.blurb: 'How do you know...', 'What does this suggest...'. Always anchor in a text quote. The most common 2-mark loss.

phenomena.label: Vocab-in-context
phenomena.blurb: Read the whole sentence and the one before. Suggest a synonym that fits. Never define a word with its own root.

phenomena.label: Language use (technique+effect)
phenomena.blurb: Name the technique (simile, metaphor, personification, repetition) AND explain the effect on the reader. Always two parts.

phenomena.label: Personal response
phenomena.blurb: 'Do you agree...'. Take a clear stance — never fence-sit. Anchor reasons in text evidence; add your own reasoning.

phenomena.label: Cloze grammar clue
phenomena.blurb: Sentence structure tells you what part of speech is needed. Find that first — it eliminates half the wrong answers.

phenomena.label: Cloze context clue
phenomena.blurb: Read the sentence before and after. Meaning narrows the choice. 'Hungry' if they couldn't wait to eat.

phenomena.label: Cloze collocation clue
phenomena.blurb: Verb + noun pairings. 'She made a _____ to volunteer' → decision (not thought).

phenomena.label: Editing — spelling errors
phenomena.blurb: Receive (i before e except after c), separately, occurred (double r), embarrass (double r AND s), necessary, definitely.

phenomena.label: Editing — 6 grammar areas
phenomena.blurb: Tense, SVA, article, pronoun, adj/adv, preposition. Identify which BEFORE writing the fix.

phenomena.label: Editing — 1 mark = both right
phenomena.blurb: Identifying the wrong word + writing the correctly-spelled correction. Mis-spell the fix and lose the mark even if right.

phenomena.label: Despite vs Although
phenomena.blurb: Despite + noun phrase ('Despite the rain'). Although + clause ('Although it was raining'). Never mix.

phenomena.label: Active ↔ Passive voice
phenomena.blurb: Object becomes subject; agent introduced with 'by'. The dog chased the cat → The cat was chased by the dog.

phenomena.label: Reported speech
phenomena.blurb: Shift BOTH tense AND pronouns. 'I am late' → 'She said that she WAS late'. Pronoun shift trips most students.

phenomena.label: Inversion (Hardly / No sooner)
phenomena.blurb: Hardly had X when Y. No sooner had X than Y. Scarcely had X when Y. Aux + subject + past participle.

phenomena.label: If-conditionals
phenomena.blurb: Type 1: present + will. Type 2: past + would. Type 3: past perfect + would have. Match the time frame.

phenomena.label: Relative clauses
phenomena.blurb: Who/which/that/whose. 'The boy IS my brother. He IS wearing red' → 'The boy WHO IS wearing red is my brother'.

phenomena.label: Almost-right distractor
phenomena.blurb: MCQ distractor that changes ONE detail: a number, a name, a date. Compare every option word-for-word against the visual.

phenomena.label: Purpose vs audience
phenomena.blurb: Purpose = what the visual DOES (persuade, inform, warn, instruct). Audience = WHO it speaks to. Don't confuse them.

phenomena.label: Typography choices
phenomena.blurb: BLOCK LETTERS = urgency/attention. Red = warning. Cartoon style = family-friendly audience.

phenomena.label: PACT (preamble framework)
phenomena.blurb: Purpose · Audience · Context · Tone. New in 2025 — decoded BEFORE reading the passage. Tone drives everything.

phenomena.label: REAP (delivery framework)
phenomena.blurb: Rhythm (pauses) · Expression (pitch) · Articulation (-ed -s -th endings) · Pace (varied). After PACT, deliver with REAP.

phenomena.label: The Reading Aloud preamble
phenomena.blurb: Short framing line that precedes the passage in 2025+. Tells you who/why. Read it FIRST; decide the tone.

phenomena.label: Silent letters (-k, -w, -ed)
phenomena.blurb: Knight = 'nite'. Wrist = 'rist'. Knee = 'nee'. Articulate -ed/-s/-th endings cleanly — easy marks lost.

phenomena.label: SBC Q1: Observe → Infer
phenomena.blurb: Describe what is literally visible THEN infer what it suggests. Real-life photos reward inference over description.

phenomena.label: PEEL (Q2 primary)
phenomena.blurb: Point · Evidence · Explain · Link. Default scaffold for personal-experience questions.

phenomena.label: OREO (Q2 alternative)
phenomena.blurb: Opinion · Reason · Example · Opinion (restated). Use when you have a strong personal view ready.

phenomena.label: VBP (Q3 critical opinion)
phenomena.blurb: Validate (acknowledge the other side) · Bridge ('However…') · Persuade. Validate FIRST — signals maturity.

phenomena.label: Real-life photo stimulus
phenomena.blurb: New in 2025 (replaces drawings/posters). Reward inference: body language, eye direction, what is NOT in frame.

phenomena.label: Signal words (listening)
phenomena.blurb: However = turn. Because = cause. First/then = sequence. For example = exemplification. Track them in real time.

phenomena.label: Audio read twice (P3)
phenomena.blurb: Use FIRST listen to identify the answer. Use SECOND to confirm. Don't restart your thinking on the second listen.

phenomena.label: First 7 items have graphics
phenomena.blurb: Items 1-7 use charts, maps, picture sets. Items 8-20 are audio only. Practice both formats.

edges.reason: Comprehension language-use questions ask you to analyse the same F.A.S.T. moves you write
SBC Q1 'observe-then-infer' is the same pattern as showing emotion through F.A.S.T.
Idiom choice depends on register — some idioms are too informal for a formal letter
Verb-preposition errors are one of the six Editing grammar categories
Cloze blanks are decided by Make/Do natural pairings
R.E.F. endings deliberately avoid banned generic phrases like 'I learnt a valuable lesson'
Reported speech requires shifting tense back one step
If-conditionals depend on which tense pair you use
Cloze grammar clues often hinge on subject-verb agreement
Swap pairs are the practical mechanism for shifting register
Each Situational Writing genre has its own register default
The 'cite + explain' move in RACE mirrors the 'describe + infer' move in SBC Q1
Despite/Although is the highest-frequency S&T pattern and the most-used Composition contrast connector
Sequence connectors are the same words that act as audio signal words in Listening
PACT's Audience+Context shapes Reading Aloud tone the same way it shapes Situational Writing register
Real-life photos are the SBC Q1 stimulus
SBC Q3 (VBP) and Comprehension Personal-Response both reward validating the opposing view
Inferential comprehension and SBC Q1 inference share the same underlying skill
The mentor essays are saturated with F.A.S.T. — read them to calibrate
2 of 3 mentor essays use Weather Contrast
1 of 3 mentor essays (The Bravest Thing) uses Flashback
All 3 mentor essay endings demonstrate R.E.F. landed well
